name: Publish social posts with MySQL and WordPress

parameters.content:
### Daily Schedule

Starts the workflow on a timer — daily at 6:00. No input data; downstream nodes fetch what they need.

Next: Read Content Calendar (MySQL)

### Read Content Calendar (MySQL)

Selects rows from the MySQL table (`returnAll`) — the read side of the store.

Next: Due for Publishing?

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 MySQL connection

### Due for Publishing?

Drops items that fail the condition; only qualifying items continue (no false branch).

Next: Post One at a Time

### Post One at a Time

Loop controller — releases 1 item(s) per pass. Output 2 (loop) processes the batch, output 1 (done) fires after the last batch.

Ports: ①→ Count Published · ②→ Publish to Slack

nodes.name: Note: Post One at a Time

parameters.content:
### Publish to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#content`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

Next: Space Out Posts

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Space Out Posts

Pauses 30 seconds — spacing calls to respect rate limits.

Next: Mark Row Published

### Mark Row Published

Inserts a row into the MySQL table using fields auto-mapped from the incoming item.

Next: Post One at a Time

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 MySQL connection

### Count Published

Collapses all items into a single summary item (for counts and run stats).

# Publish social posts with MySQL and WordPress

**Pattern:** Social Scheduler · **Level:** Foundation · **Trigger:** Scheduled

## What it does
Publish social posts with MySQL and WordPress. It runs automatically on a schedule.

## Why it exists
Manual social copy is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Daily Schedule → Read Content Calendar (MySQL) → Due for Publishing? → Post One at a Time → Count Published

## Use it for
- Teams that want to social scheduler without manual effort
- Anyone automating social copy in content creation
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Has retries and error handling built in
- Runs 24/7 in the background
- Standardizes the process every time

## Before first run
Connect credentials: MySQL connection; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

Posts go out one at a time through a batch-of-1 loop with a Wait between each, not all at once — most social APIs rate-limit bursts, and spacing also just reads as more natural than five posts landing in the same second. A row is marked published only after the post succeeds, so a mid-run crash resumes cleanly instead of re-posting everything.

## Practice exercises

New to this pattern? Try these in order:

1. Change the spacing delay and see how total run time scales with calendar size.
2. Add a second platform and post to both for each due item.
3. Manually mark one row as already-published and confirm the filter skips it.

## 🛟 When this fails

This starter workflow keeps things simple: no automatic retry or error branch yet. If a node fails, the execution just stops — open n8n's **Executions** tab to see which node and why. Ready to harden it? Turn on **Retry On Fail** (3 tries, 2s backoff) on the Settings tab of each external node, and add an error-output branch to a Slack/email alert — every Production-tier template in this library shows that exact pattern.

## ⏱ Rate limiting

Work is batched and spaced with Wait so provider quotas aren't tripped. If you hit 429s, lower the batch size or raise the wait — throughput drops, reliability rises.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your social copy process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
